lesson_title: Pure virtual functions, abstract base classes, and interface classes

cards.text: To declare a pure virtual function, add {{c1::= 0}} after the function declaration: virtual void func() {{c1::= 0}};
cards.source: 25.7 - Pure virtual functions, abstract base classes, and interface classes

cards.back: A virtual function that has no body and must be overridden by derived classes (declared with = 0)
cards.source: 25.7 - Pure virtual functions, abstract base classes, and interface classes

cards.back: A class that has at least one pure virtual function and cannot be instantiated
cards.source: 25.7 - Pure virtual functions, abstract base classes, and interface classes

cards.front: Can you create an object of an abstract base class?
cards.back: No. Any class with at least one pure virtual function is abstract and cannot be instantiated. You can only create objects of concrete derived classes that override all pure virtual functions.
cards.source: 25.7 - Pure virtual functions, abstract base classes, and interface classes

cards.question: Will this code compile?
cards.explanation: Animal is an abstract base class because it has a pure virtual function. You cannot instantiate abstract classes directly.
cards.source: 25.7 - Pure virtual functions, abstract base classes, and interface classes

cards.text: A derived class must {{c1::override all}} pure virtual functions from its base class to become a {{c2::concrete}} (non-abstract) class.
cards.source: 25.7 - Pure virtual functions, abstract base classes, and interface classes

cards.back: A class with no member variables and all pure virtual functions, defining a contract that derived classes must implement
cards.source: 25.7 - Pure virtual functions, abstract base classes, and interface classes

cards.question: Is this a valid interface class in C++?
cards.answer: Yes, this is a valid interface class.
cards.explanation: An interface class has all pure virtual functions (except possibly a virtual destructor) and no member variables. It defines a contract for derived classes.
cards.source: 25.7 - Pure virtual functions, abstract base classes, and interface classes

cards.front: What is the common naming convention for interface classes in C++?
cards.back: Prefix the class name with 'I' (e.g., ISerializable, IErrorLog, IDrawable).
cards.source: 25.7 - Pure virtual functions, abstract base classes, and interface classes

cards.concept: When to use pure virtual functions
cards.explanation:
Use pure virtual functions when:
  • A base class has no sensible default implementation
  • You want to force derived classes to provide their own implementation
  • You want to prevent instantiation of the base class
  • You're defining an interface or contract
This ensures derived classes don't forget to override critical functions.
examples: virtual double area() = 0;  // No default for area
virtual void draw() = 0;    // Must be implemented
cards.source: 25.7 - Pure virtual functions, abstract base classes, and interface classes

cards.front: Should an abstract base class have a virtual destructor?
cards.back: Yes, always. Even though the class is abstract, derived objects will be deleted through base pointers, so a virtual destructor is essential to prevent memory leaks.
cards.source: 25.7 - Pure virtual functions, abstract base classes, and interface classes

cards.question: Will this code compile?
cards.explanation: Derived doesn't override the pure virtual function func(), so Derived is also abstract and cannot be instantiated.
cards.source: 25.7 - Pure virtual functions, abstract base classes, and interface classes

cards.explanation:
Interface classes provide:
  • Clear contracts defining what derived classes must implement
  • Multiple inheritance of interfaces (safe, unlike implementation inheritance)
  • Decoupling - code depends on interfaces, not concrete implementations
  • Testability - easy to create mock implementations
They're a key tool for writing flexible, maintainable code.
cards.source: 25.7 - Pure virtual functions, abstract base classes, and interface classes
